[You're a team leader. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
Your main responsibilities will include guiding the process design expert through process generation, process refinement, and so on.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

Problem: The temperature-management path has a logic error. The process measures temperature at `activity6`, but the required response to fever (`activity7`–`activity10`) and repeated temperature monitoring every 4 hours for 48 hours (`activity11`, `activity34`) are placed only inside the admitted-patient branch under `exclusivegateway2`. This means a patient with an abnormal temperature in the ED but not admitted has no immediate fever-management path.

Suggested modification: Move the fever decision and treatment logic to directly follow `activity6` in the temperature branch, for example:
- after `activity6`, add an `exclusiveGateway`
  - branch condition=`temperature > 37.5 C`
    - initiate temperature-reducing measures
    - investigate potential infection
    - use an `inclusiveGateway` for:
      - antipyretic therapy if required
      - antimicrobial therapy if required
  - branch condition=`temperature <= 37.5 C`
    - continue routine care
- keep the “every 4 hours for first 48 hours” monitoring only for patients who remain under hospital care/admission, and let that monitoring feed into the same fever-management logic if fever develops later.

Reason: According to Module 2.2.1, temperature should be evaluated routinely, and whenever temperature is `> 37.5 C`, treatment and infection workup should begin. The current design delays these actions until after admission, which is inconsistent with the guideline and leaves non-admitted patients without the required fever-management step.

[2026-08-04 21:13:11 INFO] **[Seminar Conclusion]**:

 Problem: The temperature-management path has a logic error. The process measures temperature at `activity6`, but the required response to fever (`activity7`–`activity10`) and repeated temperature monitoring every 4 hours for 48 hours (`activity11`, `activity34`) are placed only inside the admitted-patient branch under `exclusivegateway2`. This means a patient with an abnormal temperature in the ED but not admitted has no immediate fever-management path.

Suggested modification: Move the fever decision and treatment logic to directly follow `activity6` in the temperature branch, for example:
- after `activity6`, add an `exclusiveGateway`
  - branch condition=`temperature > 37.5 C`
    - initiate temperature-reducing measures
    - investigate potential infection
    - use an `inclusiveGateway` for:
      - antipyretic therapy if required
      - antimicrobial therapy if required
  - branch condition=`temperature <= 37.5 C`
    - continue routine care
- keep the “every 4 hours for first 48 hours” monitoring only for patients who remain under hospital care/admission, and let that monitoring feed into the same fever-management logic if fever develops later.

Reason: According to Module 2.2.1, temperature should be evaluated routinely, and whenever temperature is `> 37.5 C`, treatment and infection workup should begin. The current design delays these actions until after admission, which is inconsistent with the guideline and leaves non-admitted patients without the required fever-management step.

| **task_prompt** | Module 2: Early Treatment This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). Module 2 Recommended Practices  2.1 Glucose management 2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately 2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first random glucose value is &gt; 11 mmol/L 2.2 Body temperature 2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for first 48 hours For temperature &gt; 37.5 C: • initiate temperature-reducing measures • investigate potential infection • initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required 2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) 2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke 2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed: • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high- risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset, followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends that: o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinicc o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of the location of initial presentation o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial presentation • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation, followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset 2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital 2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage: • ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81– 325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of 81 mg/day • clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day • extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with ASA 160 mg first) 2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as for patients who fail swallowing screening 2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery atherosclerosis with &gt; 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event. bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests. dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus. eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. Module 2 Implementation Considerations General considerations • Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED • Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department. |

ED, emergency department.', 'suggestions': 'Problem: The temperature-management path has a logic error. The process measures temperature at activity6, but the required response to fever (activity7–activity10) and repeated temperature monitoring every 4 hours for 48 hours (activity11, activity34) are placed only inside the admitted-patient branch under exclusivegateway2. This means a patient with an abnormal temperature in the ED but not admitted has no immediate fever-management path.\n\nSuggested modification: Move the fever decision and treatment logic to directly follow activity6 in the temperature branch, for example:\n- after activity6, add an exclusiveGateway\n  - branch condition=temperature &gt; 37.5 C\n    - initiate temperature-reducing measures\n    - investigate potential infection\n    - use an inclusiveGateway for:\n      - antipyretic therapy if required\n      - antimicrobial therapy if required\n  - branch condition=temperature &lt;= 37.5 C\n    - continue routine care\n- keep the “every 4 hours for first 48 hours” monitoring only for patients who remain under hospital care/admission, and let that monitoring feed into the same fever-management logic if fever develops later.\n\nReason: According to Module 2.2.1, temperature should be evaluated routinely, and whenever temperature is &gt; 37.5 C, treatment and infection workup should begin. The current design delays these actions until after admission, which is inconsistent with the guideline and leaves non-admitted patients without the required fever-management step.', 'format': "\nWhen the process starts, please output ''\nAn activity is represented as follows:\n\nThe exclusive gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nThe parallel gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nThe inclusive gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nAt the end of the process, please output ''\n\nThere are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:\n    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.\n    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.\n    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.\n    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.\n    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.\n    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.\n    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.\n    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.\n", 'examples': ' '} |

[2026-08-04 21:13:11 INFO] Process design reviewer: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design expert. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include process generation, process refinement, process review and so on, guided by team leader.
You model the process of a clinical practice guideline to a clinical pathway.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department.
process text: 
<process>
  <activity role='ED team' action='activate a standardized priority protocol for suspected TIA or minor nondisabling stroke' objects='patient routing and urgent stroke workflow' id='activity1_1'/>
  <activity role='ED team' action='perform initial assessment for suspected TIA or minor nondisabling stroke' objects='patient' id='activity1_2'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='measure random blood glucose' objects='blood glucose sample' id='activity2'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
        <branch condition='first random glucose indicates hypoglycemia' id='branch2'>
          <activity role='ED team' action='correct hypoglycemia immediately' objects='hypoglycemia treatment' id='activity3'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='first random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L' id='branch3'>
          <activity role='ED nurse' action='repeat blood glucose measurement' objects='blood glucose sample' id='activity4'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='first random glucose is not hypoglycemia and is <= 11 mmol/L' id='branch4'>
          <activity role='ED team' action='continue early treatment after documenting glucose status' objects='glucose result' id='activity5'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch5'>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='evaluate body temperature as part of initial routine vital signs in the ED' objects='initial vital signs record' id='activity6'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch10'>
      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway2'>
        <branch id='branch26'>
          <activity role='stroke assessment team' action='assess high-risk TIA features or minor stroke characteristics and identify fluctuating or crescendo symptoms' objects='clinical history and neurological examination findings' id='activity12_1'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch27'>
          <activity role='stroke assessment team' action='determine time from symptom onset and assess access to specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic care' objects='symptom onset timeline and referral context' id='activity12_2'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch28'>
          <activity role='stroke assessment team' action='review current antiplatelet use and assess swallowing status' objects='medication history and swallowing screen result' id='activity12_3'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='TIA with high-risk features or minor stroke' id='branch11'>
          <activity role='stroke assessment team' action='classify the patient as having TIA with high-risk features or minor stroke' objects='risk stratification result' id='activity12_4'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway9'>
            <branch condition='fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty is present' id='branch33'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='complete ED reassessment for fluctuating or crescendo motor, sensory, or speech symptoms' objects='patient with high-risk fluctuating symptoms' id='activity13_1'/>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway7'>
                <branch condition='admission to hospital is indicated' id='branch29'>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='arrange urgent brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset' objects='brain CT order and scan' id='activity13_2a'/>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='admit the patient to hospital for inpatient evaluation and stroke-prevention management' objects='admission order and inpatient management plan' id='activity13_2'/>
                  <activity role='inpatient nursing team' action='monitor body temperature every 4 hours for first 48 hours after admission' objects='inpatient vital signs record' id='activity11'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
                    <branch condition='temperature > 37.5 C during the first 48 hours after admission' id='branch6'>
                      <activity role='inpatient team' action='initiate temperature-reducing measures' objects='temperature-reducing interventions' id='activity7'/>
                      <activity role='inpatient team' action='investigate potential infection' objects='infection workup' id='activity8'/>
                      <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
                        <branch condition='antipyretic therapy is required' id='branch7'>
                          <activity role='physician' action='initiate antipyretic therapy' objects='antipyretic medication' id='activity9'/>
                        </branch>
                        <branch condition='antimicrobial therapy is required' id='branch8'>
                          <activity role='physician' action='initiate antimicrobial therapy' objects='antimicrobial medication' id='activity10'/>
                        </branch>
                      </inclusiveGateway>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='temperature remains <= 37.5 C during the first 48 hours after admission' id='branch9'>
                      <activity role='inpatient nursing team' action='continue scheduled temperature monitoring every 4 hours for first 48 hours after admission' objects='inpatient vital signs record' id='activity34'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='admission to hospital is not indicated' id='branch30'>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='continue expedited noninpatient stroke-prevention management' objects='ambulatory management plan' id='activity13_3'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway10'>
                    <branch condition='medical attention was sought within 48 hours from symptom onset and no delay to a specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic poses risk' id='branch35'>
                      <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic' action='provide immediate care' objects='patient' id='activity14_1'/>
                      <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic' action='arrange brain CT scan and antiplatelet initiation as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset provided it is not contraindicated' objects='brain CT scan and antiplatelet plan' id='activity15_1'/>
                      <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic' action='tailor other stroke-prevention treatments to the patient' objects='stroke-prevention treatments' id='activity16_1'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='medical attention was sought within 48 hours from symptom onset and delay to a specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic poses risk to patient health' id='branch36'>
                      <activity role='appropriately resourced ED' action='perform evaluation' objects='patient' id='activity17_1'/>
                      <activity role='ED physician' action='consider inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention' objects='admission decision and stroke-prevention plan' id='activity18_1'/>
                      <activity role='appropriately resourced ED' action='arrange brain CT scan and antiplatelet initiation as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset provided it is not contraindicated' objects='brain CT scan and antiplatelet plan' id='activity19_1'/>
                      <activity role='ED team' action='tailor other stroke-prevention treatments to the patient' objects='stroke-prevention treatments' id='activity20_1'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='medical attention was sought more than 48 hours after symptom onset' id='branch37'>
                      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway12'>
                        <branch condition='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic is available within 24 hours of initial presentation' id='branch38'>
                          <activity role='ED team' action='refer the patient to a specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic within 24 hours of initial presentation' objects='urgent specialized clinic referral' id='activity21_3'/>
                        </branch>
                        <branch condition='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic is not available within 24 hours of initial presentation' id='branch39'>
                          <activity role='ED team' action='refer the patient to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services within 24 hours of initial presentation' objects='urgent outpatient stroke-prevention referral' id='activity21_4'/>
                        </branch>
                      </exclusiveGateway>
                      <activity role='receiving stroke-prevention service' action='arrange urgent brain CT scan and stroke-prevention evaluation' objects='brain CT scan and stroke-prevention assessment' id='activity22_1'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty is not present' id='branch34'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='proceed with location and timing-based routing for high-risk TIA or minor stroke' objects='routing decision context' id='activity13_4'/>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway11'>
                <branch condition='medical attention was sought within 48 hours from symptom onset and no delay to a specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic poses risk' id='branch40'>
                  <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic' action='provide immediate care' objects='patient' id='activity14_2'/>
                  <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic' action='arrange brain CT scan and antiplatelet initiation as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset provided it is not contraindicated' objects='brain CT scan and antiplatelet plan' id='activity15_2'/>
                  <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic' action='tailor other stroke-prevention treatments to the patient' objects='stroke-prevention treatments' id='activity16_2'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='medical attention was sought within 48 hours from symptom onset and delay to a specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic poses risk to patient health' id='branch41'>
                  <activity role='appropriately resourced ED' action='perform evaluation' objects='patient' id='activity17_2'/>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='consider inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention' objects='admission decision and stroke-prevention plan' id='activity18_2'/>
                  <activity role='appropriately resourced ED' action='arrange brain CT scan and antiplatelet initiation as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset provided it is not contraindicated' objects='brain CT scan and antiplatelet plan' id='activity19_2'/>
                  <activity role='ED team' action='tailor other stroke-prevention treatments to the patient' objects='stroke-prevention treatments' id='activity20_2'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='medical attention was sought more than 48 hours after symptom onset' id='branch42'>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway13'>
                    <branch condition='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic is available within 24 hours of initial presentation' id='branch43'>
                      <activity role='ED team' action='refer the patient to a specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic within 24 hours of initial presentation' objects='urgent specialized clinic referral' id='activity21_5'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic is not available within 24 hours of initial presentation' id='branch44'>
                      <activity role='ED team' action='refer the patient to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services within 24 hours of initial presentation' objects='urgent outpatient stroke-prevention referral' id='activity21_6'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                  <activity role='receiving stroke-prevention service' action='arrange urgent brain CT scan and stroke-prevention evaluation' objects='brain CT scan and stroke-prevention assessment' id='activity22_2'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='TIA without high-risk features' id='branch16'>
          <activity role='ED team' action='arrange brain CT scan and antiplatelet initiation as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation provided it is not contraindicated' objects='brain CT scan and antiplatelet plan' id='activity23'/>
          <activity role='ED team' action='refer the patient to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset' objects='outpatient stroke-prevention referral' id='activity24'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <activity role='physician' action='confirm that brain imaging results are available for review' objects='brain imaging report' id='activity25_1'/>
      <activity role='physician' action='determine whether intracranial hemorrhage has been excluded before starting antiplatelet therapy' objects='brain imaging result' id='activity25_2'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4'>
        <branch condition='intracranial hemorrhage is excluded and the patient is not on an antiplatelet agent at presentation' id='branch17'>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway5'>
            <branch condition='swallowing screen passed and ASA is selected' id='branch18'>
              <activity role='physician' action='start antiplatelet therapy immediately with ASA 160 mg loading dose followed by enteric coated ASA 81 to 325 mg daily with most patients maintained on 81 mg daily' objects='ASA regimen' id='activity26'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='swallowing screen passed and clopidogrel is selected' id='branch19'>
              <activity role='physician' action='start antiplatelet therapy immediately with clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose followed by 75 mg daily' objects='clopidogrel regimen' id='activity27'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='swallowing screen passed and extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA is selected' id='branch20'>
              <activity role='physician' action='start antiplatelet therapy immediately with ASA 160 mg loading dose followed by extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg and ASA 25 mg twice daily' objects='extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA regimen' id='activity28'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='swallowing screen failed or oral ASA administration is not possible' id='branch21'>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway6'>
                <branch condition='rectal ASA is appropriate' id='branch22'>
                  <activity role='physician' action='offer rectal ASA as necessary' objects='rectal ASA' id='activity29'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='gastric ASA is appropriate' id='branch23'>
                  <activity role='physician' action='offer gastric ASA as necessary' objects='gastric ASA' id='activity30'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='intracranial hemorrhage is excluded and the patient is already on an antiplatelet agent at presentation' id='branch24'>
          <activity role='physician' action='continue evaluation of existing antiplatelet therapy within the stroke-prevention plan' objects='current antiplatelet regimen' id='activity31'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='intracranial hemorrhage is not excluded' id='branch25'>
          <activity role='physician' action='withhold antiplatelet initiation under this TIA or minor stroke pathway and reassess diagnosis and management' objects='brain imaging result and alternative management plan' id='activity32'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <activity role='ED team or stroke-prevention service' action='perform cross-continuum secondary prevention assessment for all patients whether admitted to hospital or discharged' objects='Modules 5 and 10 secondary prevention assessment' id='activity33_1'/>
  <activity role='ED team or stroke-prevention service' action='initiate or arrange appropriate secondary prevention therapies and follow-up for all patients' objects='Modules 5 and 10 secondary prevention interventions and follow-up plan' id='activity33_2'/>
</process>
suggestion:
Problem: The temperature-management path has a logic error. The process measures temperature at `activity6`, but the required response to fever (`activity7`–`activity10`) and repeated temperature monitoring every 4 hours for 48 hours (`activity11`, `activity34`) are placed only inside the admitted-patient branch under `exclusivegateway2`. This means a patient with an abnormal temperature in the ED but not admitted has no immediate fever-management path.

Suggested modification: Move the fever decision and treatment logic to directly follow `activity6` in the temperature branch, for example:
- after `activity6`, add an `exclusiveGateway`
  - branch condition=`temperature > 37.5 C`
    - initiate temperature-reducing measures
    - investigate potential infection
    - use an `inclusiveGateway` for:
      - antipyretic therapy if required
      - antimicrobial therapy if required
  - branch condition=`temperature <= 37.5 C`
    - continue routine care
- keep the “every 4 hours for first 48 hours” monitoring only for patients who remain under hospital care/admission, and let that monitoring feed into the same fever-management logic if fever develops later.

Reason: According to Module 2.2.1, temperature should be evaluated routinely, and whenever temperature is `> 37.5 C`, treatment and infection workup should begin. The current design delays these actions until after admission, which is inconsistent with the guideline and leaves non-admitted patients without the required fever-management step.
You need to modify the process text according to the suggestions and output it.
Be careful not to change the format:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.

[2026-08-04 21:14:15 INFO] Process design expert: **Process design expert<->Process design reviewer on : ProcessReview, turn 0**

[You're a process design reviewer. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include give suggestion to the process.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department..

 <process>
  <activity role='ED team' action='activate a standardized priority protocol for suspected TIA or minor nondisabling stroke' objects='patient routing and urgent stroke workflow' id='activity1_1'/>

        </branch>
        <branch condition='first random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L' id='branch3'>
          <activity role='ED nurse' action='repeat blood glucose measurement' objects='blood glucose sample' id='activity4'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='first random glucose is not hypoglycemia and is <= 11 mmol/L' id='branch4'>
          <activity role='ED team' action='continue early treatment after documenting glucose status' objects='glucose result' id='activity5'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch5'>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='evaluate body temperature as part of initial routine vital signs in the ED' objects='initial vital signs record' id='activity6'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway14'>
        <branch condition='temperature > 37.5 C at initial ED assessment' id='branch45'>
          <activity role='ED team' action='initiate temperature-reducing measures' objects='temperature-reducing interventions' id='activity7'/>
          <activity role='ED team' action='investigate potential infection' objects='infection workup' id='activity8'/>
          <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
            <branch condition='antipyretic therapy is required' id='branch7'>
              <activity role='physician' action='initiate antipyretic therapy' objects='antipyretic medication' id='activity9'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='antimicrobial therapy is required' id='branch8'>
              <activity role='physician' action='initiate antimicrobial therapy' objects='antimicrobial medication' id='activity10'/>
            </branch>
          </inclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='temperature <= 37.5 C at initial ED assessment' id='branch46'>
          <activity role='ED team' action='continue routine care after documenting temperature status' objects='temperature result' id='activity35'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch10'>
      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway2'>
        <branch id='branch26'>
          <activity role='stroke assessment team' action='assess high-risk TIA features or minor stroke characteristics and identify fluctuating or crescendo symptoms' objects='clinical history and neurological examination findings' id='activity12_1'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch27'>
          <activity role='stroke assessment team' action='determine time from symptom onset and assess access to specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic care' objects='symptom onset timeline and referral context' id='activity12_2'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch28'>
          <activity role='stroke assessment team' action='review current antiplatelet use and assess swallowing status' objects='medication history and swallowing screen result' id='activity12_3'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='TIA with high-risk features or minor stroke' id='branch11'>
          <activity role='stroke assessment team' action='classify the patient as having TIA with high-risk features or minor stroke' objects='risk stratification result' id='activity12_4'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway9'>
            <branch condition='fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty is present' id='branch33'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='complete ED reassessment for fluctuating or crescendo motor, sensory, or speech symptoms' objects='patient with high-risk fluctuating symptoms' id='activity13_1'/>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway7'>
                <branch condition='admission to hospital is indicated' id='branch29'>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='arrange urgent brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset' objects='brain CT order and scan' id='activity13_2a'/>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='admit the patient to hospital for inpatient evaluation and stroke-prevention management' objects='admission order and inpatient management plan' id='activity13_2'/>
                  <activity role='inpatient nursing team' action='monitor body temperature every 4 hours for first 48 hours after admission' objects='inpatient vital signs record' id='activity11'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
                    <branch condition='temperature > 37.5 C during inpatient monitoring in first 48 hours after admission' id='branch6'>
                      <activity role='inpatient team' action='initiate temperature-reducing measures' objects='temperature-reducing interventions' id='activity36'/>
                      <activity role='inpatient team' action='investigate potential infection' objects='infection workup' id='activity37'/>
                      <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway2'>
                        <branch condition='antipyretic therapy is required' id='branch47'>
                          <activity role='physician' action='initiate antipyretic therapy' objects='antipyretic medication' id='activity38'/>
                        </branch>
                        <branch condition='antimicrobial therapy is required' id='branch48'>
                          <activity role='physician' action='initiate antimicrobial therapy' objects='antimicrobial medication' id='activity39'/>
                        </branch>
                      </inclusiveGateway>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='temperature remains <= 37.5 C during inpatient monitoring in first 48 hours after admission' id='branch9'>
                      <activity role='inpatient nursing team' action='continue scheduled temperature monitoring every 4 hours for first 48 hours after admission' objects='inpatient vital signs record' id='activity34'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='admission to hospital is not indicated' id='branch30'>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='continue expedited noninpatient stroke-prevention management' objects='ambulatory management plan' id='activity13_3'/>

                    </branch>

                      <activity role='ED team' action='tailor other stroke-prevention treatments to the patient' objects='stroke-prevention treatments' id='activity20_1'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='medical attention was sought more than 48 hours after symptom onset' id='branch37'>
                      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway12'>
                        <branch condition='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic is available within 24 hours of initial presentation' id='branch38'>
                          <activity role='ED team' action='refer the patient to a specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic within 24 hours of initial presentation' objects='urgent specialized clinic referral' id='activity21_3'/>
                        </branch>
                        <branch condition='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic is not available within 24 hours of initial presentation' id='branch39'>

                        </branch>
                      </exclusiveGateway>
                      <activity role='receiving stroke-prevention service' action='arrange urgent brain CT scan and stroke-prevention evaluation' objects='brain CT scan and stroke-prevention assessment' id='activity22_1'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty is not present' id='branch34'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='proceed with location and timing-based routing for high-risk TIA or minor stroke' objects='routing decision context' id='activity13_4'/>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway11'>
                <branch condition='medical attention was sought within 48 hours from symptom onset and no delay to a specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic poses risk' id='branch40'>
                  <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic' action='provide immediate care' objects='patient' id='activity14_2'/>
                  <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic' action='arrange brain CT scan and antiplatelet initiation as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset provided it is not contraindicated' objects='brain CT scan and antiplatelet plan' id='activity15_2'/>
                  <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic' action='tailor other stroke-prevention treatments to the patient' objects='stroke-prevention treatments' id='activity16_2'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='medical attention was sought within 48 hours from symptom onset and delay to a specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic poses risk to patient health' id='branch41'>

                  <activity role='ED physician' action='consider inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention' objects='admission decision and stroke-prevention plan' id='activity18_2'/>

                </branch>

                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway13'>

                      <activity role='ED team' action='refer the patient to a specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic within 24 hours of initial presentation' objects='urgent specialized clinic referral' id='activity21_5'/>
                    </branch>

                      <activity role='ED team' action='refer the patient to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services within 24 hours of initial presentation' objects='urgent outpatient stroke-prevention referral' id='activity21_6'/>

            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='TIA without high-risk features' id='branch16'>

      </exclusiveGateway>
      <activity role='physician' action='confirm that brain imaging results are available for review' objects='brain imaging report' id='activity25_1'/>
      <activity role='physician' action='determine whether intracranial hemorrhage has been excluded before starting antiplatelet therapy' objects='brain imaging result' id='activity25_2'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4'>
        <branch condition='intracranial hemorrhage is excluded and the patient is not on an antiplatelet agent at presentation' id='branch17'>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway5'>
            <branch condition='swallowing screen passed and ASA is selected' id='branch18'>
              <activity role='physician' action='start antiplatelet therapy immediately with ASA 160 mg loading dose followed by enteric coated ASA 81 to 325 mg daily with most patients maintained on 81 mg daily' objects='ASA regimen' id='activity26'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='swallowing screen passed and clopidogrel is selected' id='branch19'>
              <activity role='physician' action='start antiplatelet therapy immediately with clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose followed by 75 mg daily' objects='clopidogrel regimen' id='activity27'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='swallowing screen passed and extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA is selected' id='branch20'>
              <activity role='physician' action='start antiplatelet therapy immediately with ASA 160 mg loading dose followed by extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg and ASA 25 mg twice daily' objects='extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA regimen' id='activity28'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='swallowing screen failed or oral ASA administration is not possible' id='branch21'>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway6'>
                <branch condition='rectal ASA is appropriate' id='branch22'>
                  <activity role='physician' action='offer rectal ASA as necessary' objects='rectal ASA' id='activity29'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='gastric ASA is appropriate' id='branch23'>
                  <activity role='physician' action='offer gastric ASA as necessary' objects='gastric ASA' id='activity30'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='intracranial hemorrhage is excluded and the patient is already on an antiplatelet agent at presentation' id='branch24'>
          <activity role='physician' action='continue evaluation of existing antiplatelet therapy within the stroke-prevention plan' objects='current antiplatelet regimen' id='activity31'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='intracranial hemorrhage is not excluded' id='branch25'>
          <activity role='physician' action='withhold antiplatelet initiation under this TIA or minor stroke pathway and reassess diagnosis and management' objects='brain imaging result and alternative management plan' id='activity32'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <activity role='ED team or stroke-prevention service' action='perform cross-continuum secondary prevention assessment for all patients whether admitted to hospital or discharged' objects='Modules 5 and 10 secondary prevention assessment' id='activity33_1'/>

| **task_prompt** | Module 2: Early Treatment This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). Module 2 Recommended Practices  2.1 Glucose management 2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately 2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first random glucose value is &gt; 11 mmol/L 2.2 Body temperature 2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for first 48 hours For temperature &gt; 37.5 C: • initiate temperature-reducing measures • investigate potential infection • initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required 2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) 2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke 2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed: • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high- risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset, followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends that: o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinicc o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of the location of initial presentation o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial presentation • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation, followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset 2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital 2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage: • ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81– 325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of 81 mg/day • clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day • extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with ASA 160 mg first) 2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as for patients who fail swallowing screening 2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery atherosclerosis with &gt; 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event. bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests. dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus. eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. Module 2 Implementation Considerations General considerations • Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED • Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department. |
